IsA: It is the first pictorial representation of a scene of prehistoric life based on evidence from fossil reconstructions.
It is a genre now known as paleoart.
HasA: It has a split level view.
It has figures that were numbered in later versions.
It has a large three-dimensional diorama based on it.
HasFeatureOf: It is characterized by depictions of creatures in violent interaction.
It is characterized by a split level view showing action above and below the water surface.
It is characterized by the omission of faeces in one later version.
UsedFor: It is used for educational purposes.
It is used for illustrating advances in palaeontology.
It is used for convincing scholars and the public that the deep past could be understood.
It is used as a prop in geology lectures.
LocatedIn: It is located in the National Museum Cardiff.
OriginatedIn: It originated in Lyme Regis, Dorset.
CreatedBy: It was created by the English geologist Henry De la Beche.
It was created by the professional artist Georg Scharf.
PublishedIn: It was published in scientific circles.
It was published in scientific and popular literature.
It was published in the popular Penny Magazine.
It was published in a French illustrated dictionary of natural history.
It was published in a book.
ProducedBy: It was produced by Georg Scharf.
It was produced by Francois Jules Pictet de la Rive.
It was produced by Robert Farren.
It was produced by Richard Bizley.
It was produced by Darrell Wakelam.
ConsistsOf: It consists of a large ichthyosaur biting into the long neck of a plesiosaur.
It consists of another plesiosaur trying to ambush a crocodile.
It consists of yet another plesiosaur using its long neck to seize a pterosaur.
It consists of ichthyosaurs depicted seizing various fish.
It consists of a couple of ichthyosaurs shown excreting faeces.
It consists of invertebrates including belemnites depicted as squid-like.
It consists of an ammonite represented as a floating creature.
It consists of more recognizable empty ammonite shells on the sea bottom.
It consists of stalked crinoids depicted in the lower right corner.
KnownFor: It is known for being the first pictorial representation of a scene of prehistoric life based on evidence from fossil reconstructions.
It is known for its split level view, an aquarium view.
It is known for depicting creatures in violent interaction.
AssociatedWith: It is associated with Mary Anning.
It is associated with Henry De la Beche.
It is associated with Georg Scharf.
It is associated with William Conybeare.
It is associated with William Buckland.
It is associated with Georges Cuvier.
It is associated with Leopold von Buch.
It is associated with Georg August Goldfuss.
It is associated with John Phillips.
It is associated with Francois Jules Pictet de la Rive.
About: It is about life in ancient Dorset.
It is about a scene from deep time.
It is about ichthyosaurs and plesiosaurs.
It is about the Lias' food chain.
It is about prehistoric creatures interacting with their environment.
It is about the vulnerability of long-necked creatures.
InfluencedBy: It was influenced by William Conybeare’s scientific description of marine reptile fossils.
It was influenced by William Buckland's conclusion about coprolites.
It was influenced by Buckland's vivid description of the Lias' food chain.
It was influenced by Georges Cuvier's drawings of prehistoric creatures.
It was influenced by Conybeare's cartoon of Buckland.
Influenced: It influenced several other such depictions that began to appear in scientific and popular literature.
It influenced Georg August Goldfuss's drawing of a scene out of the Jurassic.
It influenced John Phillips's woodcut of an elaborate prehistoric scene.
It influenced another illustration in a French illustrated dictionary of natural history.
It influenced the style of the Victorian aquarium craze.
